content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Julia Sylva 
Assistant City Attorney 
4319 East Slauson A venue 
Maywood, CA 90270 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-124 
Dear Ms. Sylva: 
July31,2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding conflict of interest provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May Chairman Flores take part in a decision regarding a conditional use permit for the sale of 
alcohol at a business located approximately one mile from La Tequila Restaurant, a tenant that 
leases his commercial property? 
2. May Chairman Flores take part in decisions regarding conditional use permits for commercial 
cannabis activities at a business located approximately one mile from Corona Sky, Inc., a tenant 
that leases his commercial property? 
CONCLUSIONS 
1. Yes. Based on the facts provided, the permit would not have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on the Chairman's interests, and thus he would not be disqualified. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 ofthe California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-124 
Page No. 2 
2. No. Based on the facts provided, the permits would have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on Corona Sky, Inc., a source of income to the Chairman, and thus he would be 
disqualified. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR 
The City of Maywood, located in the southeast portion of Los Angeles County, consists of 
1.14 square miles and is governed by a five-member City Council and a five-member Planning 
Commission, all appointed by the City Council. The Planning Commission reviews and grants all 
conditional use permits for the sale of alcohol and operation of commercial cannabis businesses in 
the City. 
The City Council appointed Chairman Flores to the Planning Commissi,on on February 10, 
2016. Chairman Flores owns numerous properties in the City of Maywood, including the two 
commercial properties at issue here. 
The first property was granted a conditional use permit for on-site consumption of alcohol in 
December of 1984 ("CUP No. I") by the Planning Commission. On February 1, 2012, Chairman 
Flores leased this property to the current tenant, La Tequila Restaurant, and the tenant assumed 
CUP No. I. The City recently received an application for a conditional use permit for the sale of 
alcohol from an applicant located approximately one mile away from La Tequila Restaurant. If 
approved, this would be 44th such permit issued to an applicant located within approximately one 
mile of La Tequila Restaurant. 
The second property was purchased by Chairman Flores in October of 1993. The current 
tenant is Corona Sky, Inc., a business engaging in commercial cannabis activity. Corona Sky 
received a conditional use permit to engage in commercial cannabis activities on or about August 
15, 2017 ("CUP No.2"). Presently, Corona Sky is one of only two businesses in the City of 
Maywood with a permit to sell marijuana. There are currently two applicants seeking such permits 
from the City, each of which are located approximately one mile from Corona Sky. If approved, 
these would be the 2nd and 3rd such permits issued to applicants located within approximately one 
mile of Corona Sky. 
Each of these permits will come before the Planning Commission for consideration and 
approval in the near future. 
ANALYSIS 
The Political Reform Act's provisions relating to conflicts of interest were designed to 
safeguard the integrity of decisions made by public officials, ensuring they are free from persuasion 
by their financial interest or interests of others who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) 
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in making, or in any way 
attempt to use the official's position to influence government decisions in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the meaning of the 
Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on one or 


File No. A-18-124 
Page No. 3 
more of the official's interests. (Section 87103; Regulation 18700(a).) Specific to the facts 
provided, Section 87103 identifies interests from which a conflict may arise, including: 
• 
An interest in a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect investment of 
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(a).) 
• 
An interest in real property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 
or more. (Section 87103(b).) 
• 
An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which 
aggregates to $500 or more within -12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).) 
• 
An interest in your personal finances and those of immediate family members. (See 
Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. 2 
Chairman Flores has interests in La Tequila Restaurant and Corona Sky as sources of 
income. He also has interests in the commercial rental properties leased to his sources of income as 
business entities and commercial real property. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Conflicts of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a 
governmental decision on a public official's interest is material. The standard for foreseeability 
varies depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision.3 For an interest that is 
not explicitly involved in a decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an official's 
interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).) Chairman Flores's interests are not explicitly 
involved in the decisions at issue. 
Materiality - Sources of Income and Business Interests 
Regulation 18702.1 (b) sets forth the materiality standard for interests in a business entity as 
a source of income or rental property where the interest is not explicitly involved in the 
governmental decision. Materiality is found if "a prudent person with sufficient information would 
find it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a 
change ... in the value of a privately-held business entity." (Regulation 18702.l(b).) Several 
examples of the types of decisions that may cause such a change can be found in Regulation 
18702.l(b). The one example relevant to the facts provided would be "[a]n increase or decrease in 
2 An effect on an official's interest in his or her personal finances is not considered separately if the decision 
also affects the official's interest in a business entity or real property (Regulation 18702.S(c)). Because the decisions at 
issue also affect Chairman Flores's business and real property interests, we do not further analyze his personal financial 
interest. 
3 An official's interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if the interest is a named party in, or 
subject of, the decision. An interest is the subject ofa proceeding if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, 
approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the interest, or if the 
decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation 18702.2(a)(l)-(6). (Regulation 1870l(a).) 


File No. A-18-124 
Page No. 4 
the amount of competition in tht? field in which the business entity is engaged." (Regulation 
18702.1 (b )(2). )4 
Materiality - Real Property 
Regulation 18702.2( a) provides a list of circumstances under which the reasonably 
foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on real property in which an official has an 
interest is material. Pertinent to the provided facts, the financial effect will be material if the 
decision: 
"(10) Would change the character of the parcel ofreal property by 
substantially altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or air 
quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value of 
the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest." 
"(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and 
consideration under the circumstances, to believe that the, governmental decision was 
of such a nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market 
value of the official's property." 
· 
(Regulation 18702.2(a).) 
La Tequila Restaurant 
Chairman Flores has an interest in La Tequila Restaurant as a source of income. He also has 
interests in the commercial property leased to La Tequila as a business entity and as real property. 
As noted above, the Planning Commission will soon consider an application for a conditional use 
permit for the sale of alcohol from an applicant located approximately one mile from La Tequila. 
While La Tequila does serve alcohol, its primary business is done as a restaurant. 
Accordingly, we do not find an increase in the amount competition where a business within 
approximately one mile of La Tequila receives a permit to sell alcohol. Moreover, you note that 
there are already 44 such permits in use within one mile of La Tequila. There is no indication that 
one additional permit for the sale of alcohol in an area already saturated with businesses permitted 
to sell alcohol would result in an increase in competition that could change the value of the La 
Tequila or Chairman Flores's rental business. 
In terms of his real property interest, we note that you have not provided any information 
indicating that issuance of an additional permit would change the character of the real property or 
its market value. 
4 We have previously advised that (I) the current level of competition in the field, (2) the proximity of the 
competitor(s) to the official's business entity, and (3) whether the entity and its competitor(s) share a similar target 
market should be considered when determining ifa decision would increase competition for materiality. (See Lucan 
Advice Letter, No. A-17-029.) 


File No. A-18-124 
Page No. 5 
Therefore, based on the facts provided, we find that the permit to be considered by ~he 
Planning Commission for the sale of alcohol would not have a reasonably foreseeable material 
effect on Chairman Flores's interests, and thus he may take part in decisions related to it. 
Corona Sky, Inc. 
Chairman Flores has an interest in Corona Sky, Inc. as a source of income. As noted above, 
the Planning Commission will soon consider applications for two conditional use permits for 
commercial cannabis activity within approximately one mile of Corona Sky, Inc. 
There are currently only two such permits in use in the City: Corona Sky's and its sole 
competitor's. Approval of one or both of the pending permits would significantly increase Corona 
Sky's direct competition in the commercial cannabis space within the City and its immediate 
proximity. 
Therefore, based on the facts provided, we find that the permits to be considered by the 
Planning Commission for commercial cannabis activity would have a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on Corona Sky, Inc., and thus the Chairman is disqualified from taking part 
in any decisions related to them. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
PVL:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
By: 
Peter V. Leoni 
Law Clerk Intern, Legal Division

alcohol at a business located approximately one mile from La Tequila Restaurant, a tenant that 
leases his commercial property? 
2. May Chairman Flores take part in decisions regarding conditional use permits for commercial 
cannabis activities at a business located approximately one mile from Corona Sky, Inc., a tenant 
that leases his commercial property?

CONCLUSION: 1. Yes. Based on the facts provided, the permit would not have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on the Chairman's interests, and thus he would not be disqualified.2. No. Based on the facts provided, the permits would have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on Corona Sky, Inc., a source of income to the Chairman, and thus he would be 
disqualified. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR

The second property was purchased by Chairman Flores in October of 1993. The current
